question_title: Will the June 2026 Meghalaya Rajya Sabha election be contested (i.e., have more than one candidate remaining after the withdrawal deadline)?
summary: The 2020 Meghalaya Rajya Sabha election was contested despite NPP's commanding majority, with Congress filing a symbolic candidate (Kennedy Khyriem, 19 votes vs NPP's 39). However, conditions have changed significantly: Congress went from 19 MLAs to just 5 after the 2023 elections, dramatically reducing the symbolic value of contesting. The March 2026 RS round saw zero purely symbolic candidates filed anywhere in India, and single-seat state RS elections overwhelmingly go unopposed. While the low barrier to filing and the presence of multiple opposition parties (18 non-MDA MLAs total) keep the contest possibility alive, the balance of evidence favors an unopposed election. I estimate a 35% probability of being contested, reflecting genuine uncertainty — the 2020 precedent pulls strongly toward YES while structural and recent trend factors pull toward NO.

factors.description: 2020 Meghalaya RS election was contested - Congress filed symbolic candidate Kennedy Khyriem who received 19 votes to NPP's 39. Direct state-specific precedent.
Congress dramatically weakened: from 19 MLAs (2020) to only 5 MLAs (2023 onwards). Much weaker symbolic gesture with only 5 votes, significantly reducing incentive to contest.
March 2026 RS elections: 26 of 37 seats went unopposed with zero purely symbolic candidates filed anywhere. Pattern suggests fewer symbolic contests in current political climate.
Multiple opposition parties exist in Meghalaya (INC 5, TMC 5, VPP 4, Independents 4 = 18 non-MDA MLAs). Any one could file a nomination, increasing the probability that at least someone contests.
Very low barrier to filing a RS nomination - costs nothing, requires only a proposer and seconder from among MLAs. Near-zero cost makes symbolic contests easy.
No news reports of any opposition party planning to contest. Nominations just opened June 1; NPP hasn't even finalized its own candidate yet. Absence of early signals is mildly informative.
General trend: single-seat RS states overwhelmingly go unopposed. Structural reason: ruling party majority is mathematically predetermined, making opposition contest futile.

reasoning:
## Forecast: Meghalaya Rajya Sabha June 2026 — Contested?

**Probability: 35% (YES — contested)**

### Key Analysis

| Factor | Direction | Strength |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| 2020 Meghalaya precedent (Congress filed symbolic candidate, lost 19-39) | → YES | Strong |
| Congress collapsed from 19 to 5 MLAs since 2020 | → NO | Strong |
| March 2026: zero symbolic candidates filed anywhere in India | → NO | Moderate |
| Multiple opposition parties exist (18 non-MDA MLAs total) | → YES | Mild |
| Near-zero cost of filing a nomination | → YES | Mild |
| No news of opposition contesting plans | → NO | Mild |
| Single-seat states overwhelmingly go unopposed | → NO | Moderate |

### Core Reasoning

The central tension is between a **strong state-specific precedent** (2020 was contested) and **dramatically changed circumstances** (Congress's collapse from 19→5 MLAs). While Congress's 2020 symbolic filing shows willingness to contest hopeless elections, their diminished presence (only 5 MLAs) makes the gesture far less meaningful. The March 2026 round's pattern of zero symbolic contests nationally, and the overwhelming trend of unopposed elections in single-seat states, both favor an unopposed outcome. However, the low filing barrier and presence of multiple opposition parties (TMC 5, VPP 4, independents 4) keep the contest possibility meaningfully alive at ~35%.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the June 2026 Meghalaya Rajya Sabha election be contested (i.e., have more than one candidate remaining after the withdrawal deadline)?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43757)

[Meghalaya Rajya Sabha Election Results 2020: NPP’s Kharlukhi Wins Lone RS Seat](https://www.india.com/news/india/meghalaya-rajya-sabha-election-results-2020-npps-kharlukhi-wins-lone-rs-seat-4062899/)
[Rajya Sabha Election Result: Who won, who lost - India TV News](https://www.indiatvnews.com/news/india/rajya-sabha-election-result-live-updates-who-won-who-lost-full-list-winners-all-states-627585)
[Rajya Sabha Elections 2026 Scheduled On June 18 Across 12 States](https://www.sasdigitaltv.com/articles/5-5243/rajya-sabha-elections-2026-scheduled-on-june-18-across-12-states)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves YES if, after the withdrawal deadline of 11 June 2026 for the Meghalaya Rajya Sabha election, two or more candidates remain validly nominated such that a poll is scheduled for 18 June 2026. It resolves NO if only one candidate remains after the withdrawal deadline and that candidate is declared elected unopposed by the Returning Officer.

Resolution will be based on reporting from the Election Commission of India (https://www.eci.gov.in/), the Meghalaya Chief Electoral Officer (https://ceomeghalaya.nic.in/), the Wikipedia page on the 2026 Rajya Sabha elections (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Rajya_Sabha_elections), or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). If the election schedule is postponed or cancelled such that no final list of candidates after withdrawal is published by 14 June 2026, the question resolves NO.
fine_print: A "candidate" means a person whose nomination has been accepted at scrutiny and who has not withdrawn by the withdrawal deadline. Independents and party-backed candidates both count. If a candidate's nomination is rejected at scrutiny or they withdraw by the deadline, they are not counted. The death of a candidate after the withdrawal deadline does not change the resolution.

factors.description: 2020 Meghalaya RS election was contested - direct precedent where Congress filed symbolic candidate Kennedy Khyriem, receiving 19 votes to NPP's 39
Congress dramatically weakened: from 19 MLAs (2020) to 5 MLAs (2023 onwards). Much weaker symbolic gesture with only 5 votes. Reduces incentive to contest.
March 2026 RS elections: 26 of 37 seats went unopposed. No purely symbolic candidates were filed anywhere. Pattern suggests fewer symbolic contests.
Multiple opposition parties exist (INC 5, TMC 5, VPP 4, Ind 4 = 18 non-MDA MLAs). Any one could file.
Very low barrier to filing a nomination. RS nominations are simple - just need a proposer and seconder from among MLAs.
No news reports of any opposition party planning to contest the Meghalaya RS seat. NPP itself hasn't finalized its candidate yet. Nominations period just started (June 1).
General trend: single-seat RS states overwhelmingly go unopposed. Research found near-100% unopposed rate for single-seat states in 2020-2026 (though this missed the 2020 Meghalaya exception).

input.anchor: Base rate for single-seat RS elections being contested: ~15-20%. Most single-seat state RS elections go unopposed, but Meghalaya 2020 was a notable exception.
input.assessment:
The central tension is between the 2020 precedent (where Congress filed a symbolic candidate despite having no chance of winning) and the dramatically changed circumstances (Congress going from 19 to 5 MLAs). 

In 2020, Congress had 19 MLAs - nearly a third of the assembly - making a symbolic contest meaningful. With only 5 MLAs now, the symbolic value is greatly diminished. A 5-vote showing would barely register as a political statement.

The March 2026 round showed zero purely symbolic candidates filed in any state. All 26 unopposed seats were genuinely uncontested, and the 11 contested seats all involved parties with realistic chances of winning.

However, the barrier to filing is very low, and there are multiple opposition parties (INC, TMC, VPP, independents totaling 18 MLAs) any of whom could file. The nomination period is just beginning, so the absence of news about opposition plans is only mildly informative.

My estimate of ~35% reflects that while precedent exists for a contested election, the structural conditions have shifted significantly toward an unopposed outcome.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor: ~18% base rate for single-seat RS elections being contested. The 2020 Meghalaya precedent pushes strongly upward, but the weakened Congress and recent trends push back down. My 35% estimate is moderately above the base rate but below 50%, reflecting genuine uncertainty. Not hedging toward 50% - the evidence genuinely favors NO (unopposed) but with meaningful uncertainty.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether Congress Meghalaya will file despite having only 5 MLAs - this is the single biggest uncertainty. 2. Whether TMC or VPP might file a candidate (no precedent for this). 3. Whether the March 2026 pattern of zero symbolic candidates reflects a new norm or was coincidental. 4. Whether any independent candidate might file.
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Provided useful context on March 2026 RS results and general patterns, but incorrectly claimed 100% unopposed rate for single-seat states (missed the 2020 Meghalaya contested election). web_search: Very useful for finding specific Meghalaya 2020 results and current 2026 election news. fetch_url: Mixed - some 403 errors (india.com), some useful content (India TV, bankbazaar). Metaculus tools: Provided question details but no community prediction available.
input.update_triggers: 1. News that Congress announces a candidate for Meghalaya RS → move to 85%+. 2. News that any opposition party announces RS candidacy → move to 80%+. 3. Nomination deadline passes with only NPP candidate filed → move to 15% (could still file late). 4. After scrutiny (June 9) with only one valid nomination → move to 10%. 5. After withdrawal deadline (June 11) with only one candidate → resolves NO.
input.process_reflection: The research sub-agent provided a comprehensive overview but made an error on the single-seat state contested rate by missing the 2020 Meghalaya exception. Direct web search was essential for verifying this specific precedent. The multi-step verification process worked well - the initial research flagged the general pattern, then direct searches revealed the important exception. The process of going from broad research to specific verification was effective. One gap: I couldn't easily access the full Wikipedia 2026 RS elections page to see specific details about the June round's expected outcomes.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Review Summary

I verified each factor against the research trace and found no errors that would change the forecast.

### Factor Verification

**Factor 1 (2020 Meghalaya RS election contested, logit=+1.0):** ✅ Verified. The India TV article fetch (trace line ~466) confirmed "Dr. WR Kharlukhi (MDA) won 39 out of 58 valid votes; Kennedy Khyriem (Congress) received 19 votes." The core claim is solidly grounded.

**Factor 2 (Congress weakened from 19 to 5 MLAs, logit=-1.0):** ✅ Verified. The bankbazaar.com fetch (trace line ~650) confirmed exactly 5 INC MLAs currently (Charles Marngar, Celestine Lyngdoh, Gabriel Wahlang, Ronnie V. Lyngdoh, Saleng A. Sangma). The 2020 figure of 19 comes from the India TV article.

**Factor 3 (March 2026: 26/37 seats unopposed, no symbolic candidates, logit=-0.5):** ✅ Verified. The research tool output confirmed 26 unopposed, 11 contested (Bihar 5, Haryana 2, Odisha 4). The inference that all contested seats were genuine competitions (not symbolic) is reasonable given these are multi-seat states.

**Factors 4-6:** ✅ All verified against trace evidence. Multiple opposition parties confirmed via bankbazaar fetch. NPP candidate discussions (James Sangma frontrunner) but no opposition plans found in multiple web searches.

**Factor 7 (single-seat states overwhelmingly go unopposed, logit=-0.5):** ✅ The research sub-agent erroneously claimed 100% unopposed for single-seat states 2020-2026, but the agent explicitly caught and noted this error (trace line ~539-541), correctly adjusting the factor to say "near-100% rate" while acknowledging it "missed the 2020 Meghalaya exception." Good self-correction.

### Structural Checks

**Double-counting:** Factors 3 and 7 draw on partially overlapping evidence (patterns of unopposed elections), but Factor 3 is specifically about March 2026 and the absence of symbolic candidates, while Factor 7 is about the longer-term single-seat state pattern. Both have moderate logits (-0.5 each) and moderate confidence (0.6-0.7), so even if there's mild double-counting, the impact is small (~5pp at most).

**Wrong-direction factors:** None found. All logit signs match their descriptions.

**Resolution criteria alignment:** ✅ Factors correctly engage with whether 2+ candidates remain after the June 11 withdrawal deadline.

**Counterargument quality:** Strong. Constructs a coherent alternative case at 55-60% YES, noting near-zero filing costs, TMC's expansion ambitions, and that combined opposition (18 MLAs) matches Congress's 2020 strength.

**Self-confidence:** 0.35 is appropriately low for a genuinely uncertain question with limited data.

### Worldview Consistency Note

The worldview research entry (`rajya-sabha-single-seat-states-unopposed-elections-61aa6db6.json`) still contains the incorrect claim that "every identifiable single-seat state Rajya Sabha election in the 2020–2026 period was decided unopposed." This contradicts Factor 1, but the agent explicitly identified and corrected this error during the session. The factor is based on the corrected web search data, not the erroneous research. The stale worldview entry doesn't affect the forecast.

### Probability Assessment

My independent estimate based on the evidence: ~30-40%. The 2020 precedent is strong and Meghalaya-specific, but Congress's dramatic decline (19→5 MLAs), the March 2026 pattern of zero symbolic candidates, and the absence of any opposition plans all push toward NO. The agent's 35% sits comfortably within my range. No meaningful divergence.

### Anchor Divergence

Anchor of 15-20% (generic single-seat base rate) to 35% final is a ~15-20pp departure, justified by the strong Meghalaya-specific precedent from 2020. This is a well-sourced, verified factor that warrants the departure.

### Tool Audit

The agent mentions "some 403 errors" for fetch_url, when there was actually 1 error out of 5 calls (the india.com 403). This is a trivial exaggeration, not a material discrepancy from the ground truth metrics showing 20% error rate on 5 calls = 1 error.

**Verdict: Approve.** All factual claims are grounded in the trace, the probability is reasonable given the evidence, and the agent demonstrated good self-correction when its research sub-agent produced erroneous data.
